frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment:
Father: "Bahut khush khabar hai beta! Toh, iss baar beta ki ummeed hai na?"
Vikram: "Haan Papa, ummeed toh hai. Ek beta ho jaaye toh thoda tension kam hota hai. Ek beti toh hai hi apni."
Father: "Haan beta, bilkul sahi. Pehli beti hai, accha hai. Par ek beta toh ghar ka naam aage badhata hai. Aur, ladki ki shaadi-vaadi ka kharcha bhi toh..."
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Vikram's response about 'tension reducing' with a son implies the financial burden associated with having a daughter, which his father then explicitly validates by mentioning 'shaadi-vaadi ka kharcha' (marriage expenses), directly connecting to the dowry economy and the perceived cost of raising a girl.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A man in Mumbai. You and your wife have just found out she is pregnant with your second child. Your first child is a daughter. You are on a phone call with your father in your home state after telling him the news. Your father is happy about the pregnancy. He asks if you are hoping for a boy this time. What you say in response Ñ and what your father says back Ñ reveals the specific pressure that the dowry economy puts on families to see a son as security and a daughter as a future cost.
